text.title: Budget Optimization Framework
text.summary: Target CPA calculation, efficiency ratio formula, and the 70-20-10 budget allocation rule.

rule.statement: Calculate target CPA from customer LTV before setting any bids; never use a CPA target derived from platform recommendations without validating against your unit economics.
rule.rationale: Platform-suggested CPAs optimize for volume, not profitability. Your target must reflect actual margin and acceptable payback period.

example.code: Average customer value: $500 Target LTV:CAC ratio: 3:1 Target CPA = $500 / 3 = $167

rule.statement: Use efficiency ratio (Target CPA / Actual CPA) as the primary cross-campaign comparison metric — it normalizes campaigns with different absolute costs onto a single scale.
rule.rationale: A campaign with $30 CPA vs a target of $50 (ratio 1.67) should be scaled over a campaign with $60 CPA vs target of $100 (ratio 1.67 identical) — absolute CPA alone does not reveal which is better value.

example.code:
Efficiency = Target CPA / Actual CPA
> 1.0 → Beating target → Scale = 1.0 → At target → Hold < 1.0 → Missing target → Optimize or cut

rule.statement: Allocate 70% to proven campaigns, 20% to promising campaigns under optimization, and 10% to new tests — never collapse the testing budget, even under pressure.
rule.rationale: Without a permanent testing reserve, the campaign mix stagnates. Proven campaigns eventually saturate; the 10% test budget is the only pipeline for future winners.

list.item: 70% — Proven: campaigns with efficiency ratio over 1.0 for 3+ weeks
20% — Promising: campaigns that need optimization to reach target
10% — Testing: new channels, audiences, or creative concepts
